source_alignment.subject: Science
source_alignment.source_statement: Recognise that light appears to travel in straight lines and use this idea to explain that shadows have the same shape as the objects that cast them.
source_alignment.derived_objective_note: This pack teaches light-ray reasoning and shadow explanation using manipulable ray and object models.

objective.child_goal: I can explain shadows using straight-line light.
objective.statement: Use the idea that light travels in straight lines to explain how shadows form and why shadow shape changes.
misconceptions: Thinks shadows are objects that move by themselves.
Thinks light bends around opaque objects to fill the space.
Confuses transparent, translucent and opaque materials when predicting shadows.

vocabulary.audio_script: Opaque materials block light and can make shadows.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A line showing the path light travels.
vocabulary.audio_script: A ray is a model line showing where light travels.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: Straight yellow lines from a torch.

vocabulary.child_explanation: A dark area where light is blocked.
vocabulary.audio_script: A shadow forms where light is blocked by an object.
vocabulary.visual_anchor: A dark shape behind an opaque object.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Which material blocks the most light?
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Check opaque/transparent vocabulary before shadow explanation.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Opaque materials block light. Transparent materials let light through.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Light passes through or stops at material cards.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Turn on the ray model and watch light travel in straight lines.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Introduce ray models as explanatory diagrams.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: We draw light rays as straight lines to model the path of light.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: A torch sends straight rays across a lab bench.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: The opaque block stops some rays, so a shadow forms behind it.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Where the object blocks the light, the screen stays dark.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Straight rays stop at block edges and create a shadow zone.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Move the object closer to the torch and predict the shadow.
teaching_sequence.learning_purpose: Explore how object position changes shadow size.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Changing the object's position changes which rays are blocked.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Object slider changes shadow size on screen.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Why is the shadow the same basic shape as the object?
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Light travels in straight lines, so the blocked area follows the object's outline.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: Object outline projects to shadow outline.

teaching_sequence.child_prompt: Teach Nova why light does not bend around the card to erase the shadow.
teaching_sequence.audio_script: Could the light just curve around the card? Show me with rays.
teaching_sequence.visual_model: The companion tries a curved ray and the model corrects it.

manipulatives.purpose: Let learners manipulate light source, object and screen to explain shadow formation.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners test materials, move objects, trace rays and explain shadow size and shape.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: High-contrast ray mode, reduced-motion step frames and audio descriptions of ray paths.

manipulatives.purpose: Connect material light transmission to shadow evidence by planning fair comparisons and classifying transparent, translucent and opaque samples from observations.
manipulatives.interaction_notes: Learners choose a controlled test, predict the shadow evidence, inspect matched observation cards, sort materials and challenge a classification when the evidence is insufficient or the test was unfair.
manipulatives.accessibility_notes: Observation cards use text, patterns and audio descriptions rather than colour alone; keyboard controls, no-drag category buttons and single-switch scanning support every action, while static and reduced-motion modes use fixed test-result panels with no flicker, sweeping beams or moving shadows.

practice.interleaving_notes: Interleave materials, shadow formation and shadow-size reasoning after straight-line light is secure.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Sort transparent, translucent and opaque materials by light blocking.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: material card + light test + category label
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Use everyday examples and simple language.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Build shadows from straight rays blocked by opaque objects.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: torch + object + screen + ray trace prompt
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Keep ray diagrams clean and scientifically accurate.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Predict how object position changes shadow size.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: object position slider + predict larger/smaller shadow
variant_blueprints.review_notes: Include screen position only after object-distance items.

variant_blueprints.purpose: Explain shadow shape using object outline and straight light rays.
variant_blueprints.generation_pattern: object outline + ray diagram + explanation choices

variant_blueprints.purpose: Maintain ray, opaque and shadow reasoning through spaced review.

body.prompt: Why does a shadow form behind an opaque card?
choices: The card blocks some light rays
The shadow moves by itself
The card makes darkness
The light disappears forever

expected_answer.value: The card blocks some light rays

hints: Trace the straight rays.
Where the rays are blocked, the screen is darker.
question_variants.explanation: A shadow forms where an opaque object blocks light rays.

body.prompt: Which material is opaque?
choices: cardboard
clear glass
clean water
thin air

expected_answer.value: cardboard

hints: Opaque materials block light.
You cannot see clearly through cardboard.
question_variants.explanation: Cardboard is opaque because it blocks light.

body.prompt: Move the object closer to the torch. What happens to the shadow?
choices: It usually gets larger
It disappears because light bends
It becomes transparent
It turns into the object

expected_answer.value: It usually gets larger

hints: Watch how many rays are blocked.
Closer objects can block a wider path of light.
question_variants.explanation: When the object moves closer to the light source, it often blocks a wider spread of rays, making a larger shadow.

body.prompt: Material evidence bench 1: Most light passes through and a test-grid image is seen clearly through the sample. Which conclusion is justified?
choices: transparent in this test
translucent because every solid scatters all light
opaque because it has a surface
cannot classify without using colour
body.observation: Most light passes through and a test-grid image is seen clearly through the sample.

integrity.expected: transparent in this test

body.supported_interaction: Trace labelled rays, choose material evidence or select predictions by touch, keyboard, switch scanning, eye-gaze dwell, AAC/pointing or learner-directed adult recording; numbered ray steps and controls replace dragging, drawing and handwriting.

gamification.reward: a labelled beam-map tile for evidence-based prediction

expected_answer.value: transparent in this test

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks shadows are objects that move by themselves.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner explains shadow movement without mentioning light or object position.
repair_sequence: Move the object and watch shadow follow.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner explains shadow change from object/light position.

misconception_repairs.misconception: Thinks light bends around opaque objects to fill the space.
misconception_repairs.detect_with: Learner predicts no shadow because light curves around the object.
repair_sequence: Block selected rays at object edges.
misconception_repairs.evidence_of_repair: Learner uses straight-line light to predict shadow zone.

adaptive_support.low_sensory: Use calm lab lighting and avoid flashing rays.
adaptive_support.reduced_motion: Use static ray diagrams and step-by-step sliders.
adaptive_support.audio_first: Narrate ray paths and material tests.
adaptive_support.reading_support: Use labelled icons for opaque, transparent, ray and shadow.
adaptive_support.attention_support: Show one variable change at a time.
adaptive_support.processing_support: Allow freeze-frame and ray tracing before answer.
adaptive_support.eal_support: Preteach light, ray, shadow, opaque, transparent, block and straight.

evidence.mastery_rule: Expected requires explaining shadow formation from blocked light; secure requires using ray models to predict shadow size and shape changes.
evidence.parent_summary: Can explain how shadows form using straight-line light and opaque objects.
evidence.teacher_evidence: Uses ray diagrams, material vocabulary and cause-effect explanations for shadows.
next_actions: Interleave with reflection after shadow reasoning is secure.

qa.notes: Year 6 light-and-shadows pilot reaches 240 variants with three curated questions preserved semantically unchanged and 237 deterministic review candidates. Coverage stays within KS2 geometric optics: straight-line ray models; source-object-screen geometry; evidence-bounded transparent/translucent/opaque classification; shadow formation, outline, relative size and sharpness; controlled source/object/screen changes; fair comparisons and cautious claims. Light-bends-around-object, shadow-as-object, independent-shadow, every-material-same-shadow and unfair-test misconceptions are explicitly repaired. All three declared formats and five blueprints include high-contrast patterned labels, diagram list equivalents, keyboard/switch/no-drag, static no-flicker and reduced-clutter routes with pressure-free ray-lab missions. Selected audio descriptions require produced, human-reviewed ElevenLabs assets; browser TTS is prohibited. Independent science, teacher, SEND, accessibility, safeguarding, audio and renderer review remains required before promotion.

accessibility_policy.no_timer_or_penalty: Learners are never timed for mastery and never lose earned progress for mistakes; incorrect attempts route to calm repair, replay and retry.
accessibility_policy.equivalent_response_routes: Every child-facing task must preserve an equivalent route through touch, keyboard, switch, eye-gaze, AAC, pointing, partner-mediated or adult-scribed response where appropriate.
accessibility_policy.audio_equivalent: Audio and read-aloud support must complement visible text, visuals and replay. Child-facing narration must use produced, human-listening-approved assets before release; browser TTS is not a teaching or phonics fallback.
accessibility_policy.reduced_motion: Reduced-motion and static alternatives must preserve the same curriculum evidence, feedback and reward meaning without relying on animation.
